active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method and exact constraint Jacobians
active_paradigms.description: Switch from SLSQP to trust-constr which handles nonlinear constraints more robustly. Compute exact analytical Jacobians for constraints (boundary constraints are linear, overlap constraints have closed-form derivatives). Use sparse constraint Jacobian structure since each overlap constraint only involves 4 variables (xi, yi, xj, yj). This allows trust-constr to use interior-point or trust-region methods more effectively with accurate gradient information.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through better constraint handling and convergence
active_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr requires constraint Jacobians to be properly formatted. Use scipy.optimize.NonlinearConstraint with jac parameter. Ensure sparse structure is exploited for the 325 overlap constraints.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.shgo (simplicial homology global optimization) for guaranteed global search
active_paradigms.description: shgo is a global optimizer that uses simplicial homology to find all local minima and guarantee global optimality. It handles inequality constraints natively and explores the search space systematically rather than from random starting points. For circle packing, define the objective as negative sum of radii with boundary and overlap constraints. The algorithm builds a simplicial complex to ensure thorough exploration of the feasible region.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: global optimum rather than local minima from random restarts
active_paradigms.cautions: shgo can be slow for high-dimensional problems. Use sampling methods like 'sobol' for efficiency. Reduce the number of variables by fixing the structure (e.g., fixing some circles to corners or edges) to make the problem tractable.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with Lloyd's algorithm for structure-guided optimization
active_paradigms.description: Compute Voronoi diagram of circle centers iteratively. Each Voronoi cell defines the maximum extent a circle can grow. Use Lloyd's algorithm (relaxation) to move centers toward centroids of their Voronoi cells. After each move, expand radii to touch nearest neighbors or boundaries. This exploits the geometric structure: optimal packings have circles touching 3+ neighbors or boundaries. Alternate between Voronoi-based repositioning and radius expansion until convergence.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: geometric structure exploitation for better local optima
active_paradigms.cautions: Voronoi cells near boundaries are unbounded - clip to unit square. Handle degenerate cases where multiple points are collinear. The Lloyd iteration may oscillate, so use decreasing step sizes for relaxation.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi diagram to guide circle placement with iterative refinement
tried_paradigms.description: Start with initial circle positions. Compute Voronoi diagram of current centers. For each cell, the maximum inscribed circle within the unit square constraint gives an upper bound on radius. Place circles at centroids of Voronoi cells, then recompute. Iterate until convergence. Then use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr to fine-tune positions and radii. The Voronoi approach naturally maximizes distances between circles, leading to larger radii. Combined with final optimization, this geometric insight guides toward optimal packing.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii by maximizing inter-circle distances geometrically
tried_paradigms.cautions: Voronoi cells near boundaries need special handling - clip to unit square. The inscribed circle radius is the distance to nearest Voronoi edge or square boundary. Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with qhull options for handling degenerate cases. May need regularization for numerical stability.

tried_paradigms.description: Replace the local SLSQP optimizer with differential_evolution, a global stochastic optimizer that can escape local optima. Encode constraints as penalty functions since DE doesn't natively handle constraints. Use a large population (50-100), high max iterations (500-1000), and the 'best1bin' strategy. After DE converges, polish the solution with a local optimizer. This approach explores the solution space globally rather than following gradients from a single starting point.
tried_paradigms.cautions: DE doesn't handle constraints directly - use penalty functions with large weights (1000+) for boundary and overlap violations. Use bounds [(0.05,0.95) for positions, (0.01,0.15) for radii]. Set polish=True to refine with L-BFGS-B at the end. Use seed for reproducibility.

tried_paradigms.idea: Explicit geometric construction with corner-anchored circles
tried_paradigms.description: Construct the packing explicitly without optimization. Place 4 circles in corners with radius r_corner ≈ 0.095 (touching two edges each). Place edge circles along each side at regular intervals. Fill the interior with a hexagonal lattice pattern (rows of 5-4-5-4-5-3 circles). Calculate exact positions using tangency relationships: for hexagonal packing, vertical spacing is r*sqrt(3), horizontal spacing is 2r. Adjust radii based on available space using distance-to-nearest-constraint calculations. This is fundamentally a geometric algorithm, not optimization.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Calculate exact positions analytically. For corner circles: center at (r,r), (1-r,r), (r,1-r), (1-r,1-r). For hexagonal interior: y-spacing = r*sqrt(3), offset alternating rows by r. Use iterative radius adjustment: for each circle, set radius to min(distance to all edges/2, distance to all other circles - their radii).

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method and analytical gradients
tried_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with trust-constr, which handles nonlinear constraints better using a trust-region approach. Provide analytical gradients for both objective (negative sum of radii) and constraints. The gradient of the objective is simple: zeros for positions, -1 for each radius. Constraint gradients can be computed analytically: for boundary constraints, gradients are ±1; for overlap constraints, gradients involve the distance formula. Use the SparseConstraint structure for efficiency with 26 circles (325 pairwise constraints).
tried_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr requires constraints in a specific format. Use NonlinearConstraint with analytical jacobians. Set gtol=1e-12 and maxiter=2000. The constraint Jacobian for overlap is sparse - use sparse matrices for efficiency. Start from the best hexagonal initialization.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method for more robust constrained optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with trust-constr, which uses interior-point/trust-region methods that handle nonlinear constraints better. trust-constr maintains feasibility throughout optimization and uses a more sophisticated constraint handling mechanism. Keep the multi-start strategy but use trust-constr's hessian approximation options. The key is that trust-constr uses a barrier function approach which can navigate the constraint manifold more smoothly than SLSQP's active-set method, especially for the circular non-overlap constraints.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through better constraint handling
tried_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr requires constraints in separate format (bounds, linear constraints, nonlinear constraints). Use SparseJac to speed up Jacobian computation. Set appropriate barrier_tol and gtol parameters.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.basinhopping for global optimization with random perturbations
tried_paradigms.description: Basinhopping combines local minimization with random jumps to escape local minima. It's fundamentally different from SLSQP's gradient descent - it perturbs the solution randomly, performs local minimization, and accepts/rejects based on Metropolis criterion. Use trust-constr or SLSQP as the local minimizer. Set niter=50-100 iterations, stepsize=0.1 for position variables and 0.01 for radii. The temperature parameter controls acceptance probability - use T=1.0 to start. This explores the solution space more thoroughly than multi-start SLSQP.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: global optimum for sum of radii
tried_paradigms.cautions: Basinhopping can be slow - limit iterations appropriately. Use take_step callback to ensure perturbations stay feasible. The local minimizer must handle constraints properly.

tried_paradigms.idea: Sequential greedy circle placement with local optimization at each step
tried_paradigms.description: Instead of optimizing all 26 circles jointly, place circles sequentially using a greedy strategy. For each new circle, find the position that maximizes its radius while respecting constraints from already-placed circles. Use scipy.optimize.minimize for each placement with a single radius variable and 2 position variables. Start with large circles in corners/edges, then fill interior. This reduces dimensionality from 78 to 3 per step and avoids local minima traps. After all circles placed, do a final joint SLSQP refinement.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: each circle's radius locally while maintaining feasibility
tried_paradigms.cautions: Order of placement matters significantly - try multiple ordering strategies (corners-first, largest-first, spiral-in). Must track constraints from all previous circles. Final refinement step is crucial.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.shgo for constrained global optimization
tried_paradigms.description: SHGO (Simplicial Homology Global Optimization) is specifically designed for constrained global optimization. Unlike SLSQP which is local, SHGO systematically explores the entire feasible region using simplicial homology theory. It constructs simplicial complexes to identify all basins of attraction, guaranteeing convergence to the global optimum for Lipschitz continuous functions. Define objective as -sum(radii), constraints for square boundaries and no-overlap, bounds for all 78 variables. Use method='sobol' for sampling. This deterministic global search is fundamentally different from stochastic methods and local optimizers.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through systematic global exploration of the feasible region
tried_paradigms.cautions: SHGO can be computationally expensive; use appropriate sampling options (n=128 or higher for sobol); ensure constraints return arrays not scalars; set iters options for thoroughness vs speed tradeoff; may need to reduce constraint tolerance

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.basinhopping with constraint-aware local minimization
tried_paradigms.description: Basin-hopping combines random perturbations with local minimization and Metropolis acceptance criterion. Unlike multi-start SLSQP, it accepts worse solutions with some probability, allowing escape from deep local minima. Configure SLSQP as the local minimizer with constraints, then define a custom take_step function that perturbs positions and radii while respecting bounds. The temperature parameter controls acceptance probability - tune it based on objective scale. Run for niter=200-500 iterations. This stochastic global search explores the energy landscape more thoroughly than deterministic restarts.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through stochastic exploration of the solution landscape
tried_paradigms.cautions: Must define take_step callback that respects bounds; temperature parameter needs tuning (start with ~0.5); local minimizer must handle constraints; use disp=True for debugging; run time increases with niter; post-process solution for validity

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with COBYLA method - derivative-free constrained optimization
tried_paradigms.description: COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) is a derivative-free method that approximates constraints and objective using linear models. Unlike SLSQP which uses gradients, COBYLA constructs simplex-based approximations, potentially finding different local optima unreachable by gradient-based methods. It's particularly effective when the objective has non-smooth regions or when gradient information is unreliable. Define the same constraints (inequality form), use rhobeg parameter to control initial simplex size (~0.1), and run from multiple starting points. The different search dynamics may discover solutions SLSQP misses.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through derivative-free exploration of different local optima
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA can be sensitive to rhobeg parameter; doesn't support bounds directly - must add as constraints; may need more iterations (maxiter=3000+); solution quality varies with starting point - use multiple restarts; less precise than gradient methods - may need SLSQP refinement after
